content: The changing seasons on Earth are caused by the way our planet is tilted as it orbits the Sun. Earth is tilted at an angle of about 23.5 degrees, which means different parts of the planet get varying amounts of sunlight throughout the year. When the Northern Hemisphere leans toward the Sun, it experiences longer days and more direct sunlight, leading to warmer temperatures and summer. At the same time, the Southern Hemisphere tilts away from the Sun, resulting in shorter days, less direct sunlight, and winter. Six months later, the situation reverses, causing the seasons to switch. This tilt, combined with Earth’s yearly journey around the Sun, creates the familiar cycle of spring, summer, fall, and winter. Understanding this natural rhythm helps businesses plan for seasonal changes in consumer behavior, energy use, and agriculture, all of which are influenced by the shifting climate throughout the year.
